page: Vultr vhp-2c-4gb-amd is a virtual server instance belonging to the High Performance family. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an AMD EPYC-Rome processor with 2 shared vCPUs and 2 physical cores operating at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 4.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB per core. Storage is bundled as a 100 GB local NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No GPU accelerators are present on this system.

Benchmark data reveals a contrast between single-core and multi-core capabilities. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance is strong, placing in the top 25% with a score of 2380.94. However, multi-core CPU performance drops to the bottom 25% with a score of 4691.97, which is a common characteristic of shared CPU allocations. Memory performance is constrained, with large-block read bandwidth at 20.62 GB/sec (bottom 10%) and write bandwidth at 21.58 GB/sec (bottom 25%). Passmark CPU and memory scores fall into the bottom 25% and bottom 10% respectively, while database operations and Redis SET performance both rank in the bottom 10%.

This instance offers a low-cost entry point for workloads that require fast single-core bursts but do not demand sustained multi-core execution or high memory throughput. The local NVMe SSD provides fast storage access, but the shared CPU allocation and low memory bandwidth limit its capability for heavy database operations or high-throughput web serving. It is appropriate for lightweight applications, basic development environments, single-threaded tasks, or small-scale utility services.

However, multi-core performance, memory bandwidth, and database operations fall into the bottom 10% to 25% ranges, reflecting the limitations of its shared CPU allocation and modest memory capacity. This instance represents a budget-friendly option for workloads requiring fast single-core execution but minimal multi-threaded or memory-intensive processing. It is appropriate for lightweight utility services, basic development environments, and single-threaded tasks.

tagline: A budget-friendly shared-CPU instance combining strong single-core performance with local NVMe storage for lightweight utility workloads.
